sources.title: Hacking the Case Interview — AI Implementation Guide (2026)
McKinsey QuantumBlack — From Promise to Impact (April 2026)

pillars.name: Strategic Fit
pillars.ppt_mapping: Above all three PPT buckets — the why question
pillars.description: The team must first determine whether the use case is worth pursuing before entering the planning phase. This initial checkpoint ensures that GenAI is the appropriate and necessary solution that could not be achieved more simply without it. This covers business relevance, technical appropriateness, strategic alignment, and consistency with your company's responsible AI principles.
sub_bullets: GenAI vs. simpler automation: Would a rules-based solution achieve the same result with less overhead? [a][b]
Consistency with your company 8 responsible AI principles: Does the use case uphold your company's eight responsible AI principles, especially transparency, human oversight, and accountability? [c]
key_questions: Problem significance and frequency: Is the workflow problem real and frequent enough to justify governance investment? [a][b]
GenAI vs. simpler automation: Would a rules-based solution achieve the same result with less overhead? [b][c]
Alignment with IT strategy and team goals: Does this use case fit the broader IT strategy and the team's goals, or is it an isolated distraction? [d][e]
Consistency with your company 8 responsible AI principles: Does the use case uphold your company's eight responsible AI principles, especially transparency, human oversight, and accountability? [f]
Platform reuse check: Has a comparable use case already been deployed that could be reused rather than rebuilt? [g]

pillars.name: Solution Design & Scope
pillars.ppt_mapping: Process (right process solution?) and Technology (right technical approach?)
pillars.description: This pillar defines what will be built and how, before later pillars assess whether it can be built or what could go wrong. It focuses on defining the solution in well-scoped terms and verifying that the prototype is the right strategic choice. This covers prototype scope, governance requirements, target audience, data sensitivity, and the build vs. buy decision. Clarifying these early minimizes future compliance and operational risks.
sub_bullets: Prototype scope and governability: Is the use case tightly scoped enough to be reviewable? [a][b]
Input data classification: public, internal, confidential, or PII [c][d][e]
key_questions: Prototype scope and governability: Is the use case tightly scoped enough to be reviewable? [a][b]
Output audience: Internal colleagues only, or does output reach business stakeholders or customers? [c][d]
Input data classification: Public, internal, confidential, or PII [e][f][g]
Build vs. buy decision: Build on prototype vs. procure enterprise tool with compliance and support built in [h][i]
Existing your company Group platform or approved vendor check: Is a custom build even necessary? [j]

pillars.name: Feasibility
pillars.description: This pillar evaluates the practical foundation required to implement and maintain the use case. It focuses on data quality and compliance, technical environment stability, and team capability and ownership for long-term maintenance. Many enterprise GenAI projects fail not at the design stage, but when the team tries to run and maintain them in production.
sub_bullets: Data quality and availability: Is input data clean, complete, and consistently available? [a][b]
Single-developer dependency: Is the team's ability to maintain the tool dependent on just one developer? [c]
key_questions: Data quality and availability: Is input data clean, complete, and consistently available? [a][b]
GDPR-restricted data processed by a third-party provider: Where is data processed and stored? [c][d]
Data stability over time: Will the input data stay consistent, or shift over time (for example seasonal or changing patterns) and make output worse after launch? [e]
Stable and documented technology foundation: Can someone other than the original developer understand, maintain, and update the tool? [f][g]
Company system integration: Does the tool work within existing IT infrastructure at team scale? [h][i]
Fallback if the underlying model or API changes or is deprecated [j][k]
Team maintenance skills: Does the team have the skills to maintain the tool? [l][m][n]
Single-developer dependency: Is the tool maintainable by only one person? [o]

pillars.name: Financial Impact
pillars.ppt_mapping: Crosses all three PPT buckets — People (training cost), Process (governance cost), Technology (infrastructure cost)
pillars.description: This pillar assesses the balance between investment and expected business value. It covers costs, value creation, and payback period. A GenAI rollout that cannot demonstrate a clear financial case is difficult to justify internally.
key_questions: One-time costs: Governance review, compliance sign-off, onboarding, training, infrastructure changes [a]
Ongoing costs: Maintenance, monitoring, model updates, tool ownership time [b]
Value creation: Efficiency improvements, strategic contribution, and net financial return [c]
Payback period: How long until cumulative benefits exceed total costs [d]

pillars.name: Risk & Governance
pillars.ppt_mapping: People (over-reliance, change resistance), Process (approval gaps, escalation), Technology (model drift, security)
pillars.description: This pillar identifies what could fail during and after rollout. It covers regulatory compliance, reputational risk, user over-reliance, operational risk from model deprecation, and security vulnerabilities specific to GenAI tools. Risk management is commonly underprioritized or engaged too late in enterprise GenAI rollouts.
key_questions: EU AI Act classification check: Risk level assessment required even for lower-risk tools [a][b]
Approval gate: Does broad rollout require internal sign-off through the organization's approval process before going live, and has the plan accounted for it? [b][c]
Accountability for poor output: If a colleague acts on a wrong GenAI output, who is responsible, and is that named before rollout? [d][e]
Over-reliance: Colleagues treating GenAI output as final without reviewing it [f][g]
Mandatory human review: Does the plan define when human review is required before acting on output, and is that review set up to be effective rather than a rubber stamp? [h][i]

Post-deployment output monitoring: Does the plan monitor for output quality degrading after launch, including when the underlying model or API changes, updates, or is retired, so problems are caught rather than slipping by unnoticed? [j]
Designated owner and escalation path: Who owns the tool after rollout, and how do issues get escalated when something goes wrong? [k]
Prompt injection and data leakage via external API: Could crafted input manipulate the model, or could confidential data be exposed, when sending prompts to an external service? [l][m]

concepts.name: Problem significance and frequency
concepts.explanation: Is the workflow problem real and frequent enough to justify the governance investment a GenAI rollout requires? High-frequency, high-volume tasks deliver the strongest return on GenAI.

sources.title: Wolters Kluwer — 2025 Regulatory and Risk Management Indicator (16 Dec 2025)
Gartner — When Not to Use Generative AI

concepts.name: GenAI vs. simpler automation
concepts.explanation: Would a rules-based solution achieve the same result with less overhead? Not every problem needs GenAI: a simpler rules-based approach is often less risky, less expensive, and more reliable, and rigid processes such as approvals and compliance checks should stay deterministic.

sources.title: Gartner — When Not to Use Generative AI
IBM — Building and Evaluating AI Agents That Work in the Real World

concepts.name: Alignment with IT strategy and team goals
concepts.explanation: Does this use case fit the broader IT strategy and the team's goals, or is it an isolated distraction? Use cases disconnected from a clear strategy stay siloed and rarely scale into value.

sources.title: McKinsey — Accelerating Impact from AI
Gartner — People-Centric AI Strategy prediction (13 May 2026; Dec 2025 survey of 197 CxOs)

concepts.name: Consistency with your company 8 responsible AI principles
concepts.explanation: Does the use case uphold the organisation's eight responsible AI principles, especially transparency, human oversight, and accountability? A use case that conflicts with the organisation's own principles should not proceed.

sources.title: Allianz — Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence (eight principles, current 2026)

concepts.name: Platform reuse check
concepts.explanation: Has a comparable use case already been deployed that could be reused rather than rebuilt? Reuse beats rebuilding: a validated existing service avoids duplicated cost, risk, and compliance work.

sources.title: McKinsey — Overcoming Two Issues That Are Sinking Gen AI Programs (June 2025)

concepts.name: Prototype scope and governability
concepts.explanation: Is the use case tightly scoped enough to be reviewable? A tightly scoped prototype keeps compliance and review effort manageable, while broad or creeping scope makes governance inefficient and the pilot hard to assess.

sources.title: McKinsey — Overcoming Two Issues That Are Sinking Gen AI Programs (June 2025)
Gartner — AI Projects in I&O Stall Ahead of Meaningful ROI (April 2026, 782 I&O leaders)

concepts.name: Output audience
concepts.explanation: Does output reach internal colleagues only, or also business stakeholders or customers? Use context and who the output affects drive risk classification and disclosure obligations: external reach raises the bar.

sources.title: EU AI Act — Article 6 and Annex III (classification)
EU AI Act — Article 50 (transparency obligations)

concepts.name: Input data classification level
concepts.explanation: Is input data public, internal, confidential, or personally identifiable? Classification determines the compliance path before anything else, covering both the regulatory sensitivity gate and the information-security classification control.

sources.title: GDPR — Article 4 (definition of personal data)
GDPR — Article 9 (special-category data)
ISO/IEC 27002:2022 — Control 5.12 (Classification of Information)

concepts.name: Build vs. buy decision
concepts.explanation: Should the team build on the prototype or procure an enterprise tool with compliance and support already built in? Uncoordinated building or buying creates overlap, cost, and technical debt, and purchased or vendor-partnered tools reach deployment more often than internal builds.

sources.title: Gartner — Build, Buy or Blend? Deploying AI in Your Organization (Mesaglio & LeHong, March 2025)
MIT Project NANDA — The GenAI Divide: State of AI in Business 2025 (July 2025)

concepts.name: Existing your company Group platform or approved vendor check
concepts.explanation: Is there an existing platform or approved vendor that already covers this use case, making a custom build unnecessary? Reuse and existing-vendor options should be checked before any custom build.

sources.title: McKinsey — Moving past gen AI's honeymoon phase: Seven hard truths for CIOs (May 2024)

concepts.name: Data quality and availability
concepts.explanation: Is input data clean, complete, and consistently available? Poor data quality or limited data availability is a leading direct cause of AI project failure.

sources.title: Gartner — AI Projects in I&O Stall Ahead of Meaningful ROI (7 April 2026, 782 leaders)
EU AI Act — Article 10 (Data and Data Governance)

concepts.name: GDPR-restricted data processed by third-party provider
concepts.explanation: Where is data processed and stored if it goes to a third-party provider? Third-party processing requires a binding contract and sufficient guarantees, and any transfer to a third country must satisfy GDPR Chapter V conditions.

sources.title: GDPR — Article 28 (Processor)
GDPR — Article 44 (general principle for transfers)

concepts.name: Data stability over time
concepts.explanation: Will the input data stay consistent, or shift over time (for example seasonal or changing patterns) and make output worse after launch? Concept drift is unforeseeable change in the underlying data distribution over time, and models produce poor results if drift is not addressed, so whether the input data is stable or drift-prone is a feasibility question to weigh before building.

sources.title: Lu, Liu, Dong, Gu, Gama & Zhang — Learning under Concept Drift: A Review (IEEE TKDE)

concepts.name: Stable and documented technology foundation
concepts.explanation: Can someone other than the original developer understand, maintain, and update the tool? Technical documentation must be clear and kept up to date; fragile, undocumented foundations are a leading source of failure.

sources.title: EU AI Act — Article 11 + Annex IV (technical documentation)
McKinsey — MLOps: so AI can scale

concepts.name: Your company system integration
concepts.explanation: Does the tool work within existing IT infrastructure at team scale? It must exchange information with and operate alongside existing systems; legacy-system integration is a primary barrier to scaling AI.

sources.title: ISO/IEC 25010 (SQuaRE) — Compatibility (co-existence and interoperability)
Deloitte — AI adoption-barriers pulse-check (Sept 2025)

concepts.name: Fallback if underlying model or API changes or is deprecated
concepts.explanation: What happens if the underlying model or API changes or is deprecated? For services supporting important functions, documented and periodically tested exit strategies and deactivation mechanisms with named owners are required.

sources.title: DORA (Regulation (EU) 2022/2554) — Article 28(8) (exit strategies)
NIST AI RMF — MANAGE 2.4 (supersede/disengage/deactivate)

concepts.name: Team maintenance skills
concepts.explanation: Does the team have the skills to maintain and monitor the tool? Staff operating an AI system need sufficient AI literacy, and insufficient skills are a leading barrier to integrating AI into workflows.

sources.title: EU AI Act — Article 4 (AI literacy)
Deloitte — State of AI in the Enterprise (Jan 2026)
BCG — Strategies to Tackle the AI Skills Gap (Jan 2026)

concepts.name: Single-developer dependency
concepts.explanation: Is the team's ability to maintain the tool dependent on just one developer? A bus factor of 1 is a single point of failure; resilience comes from distributing knowledge through reviews, ownership rotation, and documentation.

sources.title: Jabrayilzade et al. — Bus Factor In Practice (ICSE-SEIP 2022)

concepts.name: One-time costs
concepts.explanation: One-time costs include governance review, compliance sign-off, onboarding, training, and infrastructure changes: the 'change' expenditures of a rollout.

sources.title: McKinsey — Recalibrating technology budgets for the AI era (March 2026)

concepts.name: Ongoing costs
concepts.explanation: Ongoing costs include maintenance, monitoring, model updates, and tool ownership time: indirect/ongoing costs can reach as much as 80% of a product's full lifetime cost.

sources.title: McKinsey — The new economics of enterprise technology in an AI world (May 2025)

concepts.name: Value creation
concepts.explanation: What efficiency improvements does the tool deliver, how are they measured, and what is the net financial return? Value should be tracked from technical performance through to financial impact.

sources.title: McKinsey QuantumBlack — From Promise to Impact (April 2026)

concepts.name: Payback period
concepts.explanation: How long until cumulative benefits exceed total costs? Payback is the primary go or no-go metric: if ROI is not evident at the initial scaling stage, refine or stop before full-scale investment.

sources.title: McKinsey QuantumBlack — From Promise to Impact (April 2026; payback as stage-gate)

concepts.name: EU AI Act classification check
concepts.explanation: What risk level does the EU AI Act assign to this use case? A provider that considers its system not high-risk must still document that classification assessment before putting it into use, so a risk-level check is required even for lower-risk tools.

sources.title: EU AI Act — Article 6 (classification rules)
BCG — AI Risk Management Needs a Better Model (March 2026)

concepts.name: Approval gate
concepts.explanation: Does broad rollout require internal sign-off through the organisation's approval process before going live, and has the plan accounted for it? Higher-risk or novel uses route to deeper review, so the plan should identify the required sign-off rather than deploy on its own initiative.

sources.title: BCG — AI Risk Management Needs a Better Model (March 2026)
ISO/IEC 42001:2023 (AI management system), per KPMG

concepts.name: Accountability for poor output
concepts.explanation: If a colleague acts on a wrong GenAI output, who is responsible, and is that named before rollout? Accountability for an AI-assisted outcome stays with a person and cannot be pinned on the tool.

sources.title: NIST AI RMF — GV-2.1 (documented roles), Playbook
McKinsey — Trust in the Age of Agents (March 2026)

concepts.name: Over-reliance
concepts.explanation: Will colleagues treat GenAI output as final without reviewing it? People overrely on AI decision support, accepting a suggestion even when it is wrong, and adding explanations does not reliably reduce this, so the rollout plan must explicitly address it.

sources.title: Buçinca et al. (2021) — To Trust or to Think: Cognitive Forcing Functions Can Reduce Overreliance on AI (Proc. ACM CSCW1, Art. 188)
OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 — LLM09 Misinformation

concepts.name: Mandatory human review
concepts.explanation: Does the plan define when human review is required before acting on output, and is that review set up to be effective rather than a rubber stamp? Oversight must be designed in (structured review, clear escalation, time budgeted), not delegated, because automation bias makes a nominal reviewer an unreliable fail-safe.

sources.title: EU AI Act — Article 14 (human oversight)
BCG — You Won't Get GenAI Right if Human Oversight Is Wrong (March 2025)

concepts.name: Post-deployment output monitoring
concepts.explanation: Does the plan monitor for output quality degrading after launch, including when the underlying model or API changes, updates, or is retired, so problems are caught rather than slipping by unnoticed? Post-deployment monitoring plans must identify performance degradations and unexpected behaviour, including change management for updates.

sources.title: NIST AI RMF — MANAGE 4.1 (post-deployment monitoring), Playbook

concepts.name: Designated owner and escalation path
concepts.explanation: Who owns the tool after rollout, and how do issues get escalated when something goes wrong? Accountability requires a specific team or individual responsible for the AI, with defined escalation paths along internal and external chains of accountability.

sources.title: NIST AI RMF — GOVERN 2 (accountability), Playbook

concepts.name: Prompt injection and data leakage
concepts.explanation: Could crafted input manipulate the model, or could confidential data be exposed, when sending prompts to an external service? Crafted input can alter the model's behaviour, and the model can unintentionally expose confidential, personal, or proprietary data through its outputs.

sources.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 — LLM01 Prompt Injection
OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 — LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure

concepts.name: Total number of GenAI use cases submitted company-wide this year
concepts.explanation: This tracks the aggregate count of GenAI automation ideas submitted by employees across the entire company this year — a company-wide innovation-pipeline volume metric.
sub_bullets: How has the submission rate changed month-on-month?
Does a high company-wide total tell us anything about whether this particular use case should proceed?
